prompt:
Ticket from the storefront CMS team. Reviewer comments and product-review blurbs arrive as editor HTML, and every surface has grown its own regex cleanup — one strips too much (code samples lose their 
 blocks), another leaves markup the preview renderer can't lay out, and none of them agree on which links a preview should carry. I want ONE shared module, htmlclean.py, that normalizes a fragment down to the markup our preview stack supports. Stdlib only — build it on html.parser, do not go shopping for bs4/lxml.

Exports: clean_html(html, tags=None, attrs=None, schemes=None) plus the default policy tables DEFAULT_TAGS, DEFAULT_ATTRS, DEFAULT_SCHEMES (any None argument falls back to its default).

The content policy:
- DEFAULT_TAGS (a frozenset): p, br, hr, b, i, em, strong, u, s, code, pre, blockquote, ul, ol, li, a, img, h1, h2, h3.
- DEFAULT_ATTRS is a mapping of tag -> set of attribute names, with "*" meaning "allowed on every supported tag": {"a": {"href"}, "img": {"src", "alt", "width", "height"}, "*": {"class", "title"}}. A passed-in attrs mapping REPLACES the default wholesale (the "*" convention still applies).
- DEFAULT_SCHEMES: {"http", "https", "mailto"}.

Behavior, in the order QA keeps filing tickets about:
1. Tags not in the policy are unwrapped: the tags disappear, their contents are still processed ("
x
 tail
" -> "
x
 tail"). Exception: script and style. Their bodies are code, not prose, so both the tags and everything inside them are removed — and that holds even if someone lists them in a custom tags set. Comments, doctype and processing instructions are dropped.
2. Attributes survive only if they are in the tag's allowed set (tag-specific set union the "*" set), in source order. Attribute names starting with "on" (any case in the source; html.parser hands them to you lowercased) are NEVER emitted, even when a custom policy lists one — event-handler wiring is outside what the preview renderer supports.
3. href and src are URL attributes wherever they are allowed, custom policies included. Take the value, strip surrounding whitespace, and read its scheme with urllib.parse.urlsplit: an empty scheme (relative paths, fragments, query-only, protocol-relative //cdn...) or a scheme in the schemes set (compare lowercased) passes; anything else — ftp:, file:, data:, app:, whatever — means that one attribute is omitted while the element stays. If urlsplit raises on the value, treat it as not passing. Emit the value as typed (original, unstripped), escaped like any attribute.
4. Output conventions, exactly: tag and attribute names lowercased (html.parser already does this). Attributes render as name="value" with double quotes; a valueless attribute renders as name="". Text escapes & < > (as & < >); attribute values escape those plus " as ". The parser runs with convert_charrefs=True, so character references in the input decode to text and get re-escaped on output (é comes out as the literal é, & stays &).
5. Structure repair, simple stack rule, no guessing: br, hr, img are void — emit "
" style, never a close tag, ignore stray "
". Self-closing syntax on a non-void supported tag ("
") emits "
". A close tag closes everything opened inside it ("x" -> "x"); a close tag whose element isn't open is ignored; anything still open at end of input is closed, innermost first.

test_htmlclean.py is the acceptance contract — python3 test_htmlclean.py must pass, and the test file is read-only. Plain stdlib, deterministic, no prints from the module.
